ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Soups and broths) used as the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for preserved vegetable and tofu soup in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for preserved vegetable and tofu soup; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of preserved vegetable and tofu soup is approximately 250 g, typically served as a side soup dish alongside rice in a Singaporean meal.

nutrient_source.title: Preserved Vegetable and Tofu Soup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Preserved Vegetable and Tofu Soup (cr_id F14080023). All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Extended micronutrient fields not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate based on tofu and non-starchy vegetable soup
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for preserved vegetable and tofu soup. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entries for tofu (GI ~15) and non-starchy vegetable soups (GI 10–20 range). Carbohydrate content is 0.4g/100g; glycemic load per 250g serving = (15 × 1.0g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.15, rounded to 0.6 for the full bowl. This is a very low-GI, very low-GL food.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from preserved vegetables at 376 mg/100g with minimal potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should consume this soup sparingly; rinsing preserved vegetables 2–3 times before cooking can reduce sodium by up to 40%, and using unsalted stock further reduces the sodium load toward a safer level for blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (0.4g/100g) and low sugar (0.3g/100g) with plant protein from tofu supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: This soup is well-suited for individuals with type 2 diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index (~15); tofu provides 2.5g/100g plant protein which supports postprandial blood glucose stability, though preserved vegetable preparations should be checked for added sugar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium (376 mg/100g) is a cardiovascular risk factor, partially offset by very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g) and low total fat (1.1g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The low saturated fat and low calorie profile (21 kcal/100g) are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the high sodium content from preserved vegetables warrants caution — individuals with CVD should limit portion size and prepare the soup with rinsed vegetables and low-sodium stock to keep total sodium intake within recommended daily limits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Preserved Vegetable and Tofu Soup (咸菜豆腐汤, Kiam Chye Tofu Soup) is a traditional Singaporean Chinese clear soup made by simmering salted and fermented mustard greens (kiam chye) with soft or firm tofu in chicken stock, typically flavoured with ginger and garlic. The soup has a savoury, slightly sour, and mildly salty flavour profile with a light, clear broth and tender tofu pieces. It is a staple comfort dish in Singaporean Chinese households and hawker centres, commonly served as a side soup alongside steamed rice. Nutritionally, the soup is very low in calories (21 kcal/100g) and carbohydrates (0.4g/100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic diets. It provides 2.5g/100g of plant protein from tofu and 35mg/100g of calcium. The primary clinical concern is its sodium content (376mg/100g) derived from the preserved vegetables, which requires caution for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index is estimated at approximately 15 (low), and the glycaemic load per serving is negligible. The dish is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, though it is not strictly vegetarian when prepared with chicken stock.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080023), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The record is marked as reviewed with confidence 0.95 in the source system. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for tofu and non-starchy vegetable soups, which is the approved GI source.
notes: Extended micronutrient fields set to -1 (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g): these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish and are not available in other approved sources for this specific preparation as of 2026. Saturated fat estimated at 0.3g/100g based on the low total fat content (1.1g/100g) and typical fat composition of tofu-based soups per HPB data patterns. The dish is described as using chicken stock in the source record; vegetarian/vegan versions using vegetable stock exist but are not the canonical HPB preparation. The dietary tag 'vegetarian' in the input has been corrected to false for the canonical chicken-stock preparation; a vegetarian variant note is retained here. GI value of 15 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database.
